finding: Performing an act of regulatory control depleted a limited metabolic resource (glucose), and this depletion impaired subsequent regulatory control attempts; replenishing the resource eliminated the impairment.
Prior effortful regulatory exertion weakened conflict-monitoring neural signals, which mediated degraded performance on a subsequent executive control task.

Exerting regulatory control on one task reduced physical stamina, hastened giving up on subsequent tasks, and impaired subsequent regulatory control in different domains—consistent with a strength-depletion model.

Initial exertion of executive control (attention, inhibition, memory, emotional expression) reduced the capacity for subsequent executive control tasks, supporting a limited-resource model of executive functioning.
Depletion of self-regulatory resources impaired subsequent performance on complex thinking requiring active self-guidance, but not on simpler mental tasks requiring no self-direction.

Maintaining important secrets was experienced as a physical burden, leading to perception of hills as steeper and reduced willingness to help with physical tasks—effects modulated by secret burden and cognitive preoccupation.
Primes of money elicited self-sufficient orientation, reducing both requests for help and helpfulness to others, and increasing preference for solitude and physical distance from others.

Blood glucose levels regulated temporal discounting of future rewards: sugar intake reduced discounting (made people more patient), while artificial sweetener increased discounting (made people more impatient).
Activation spreads continuously from conceptual to phonological representations during word production, but the magnitude of activation transfer is limited and modulated by task demands.
Repetition blindness for repeated items depended on participant strategy: when guessing from partial information was discouraged, repetition blindness occurred for both words and nonwords.
Switching between tasks imposed additional demands on working memory capacity, independent of the concurrent load of maintaining items in memory.
Items that had undergone retrieval-induced forgetting benefited more from relearning than items that had not been forgotten, suggesting that forgetting enables faster subsequent learning.
Better discrimination of intermixed versus blocked similar stimuli was not explained by differences in trial spacing, suggesting an alternative associative-learning mechanism.
Priming, recognition, and fluency effects can emerge from a single system rather than requiring multiple systems, as demonstrated through computational modeling.
A single computational model predicted appropriate distributions of effect sizes for phonological and word-length effects without invoking changes in encoding strategy or storage mechanisms.

Activated stereotypes at retrieval spontaneously generated illusory recollections consistent with expectations, as people manufactured recollective experiences from both memory traces and retrieval-context information.

Numbers in different notational systems were automatically translated into a common magnitude representation, allowing cross-notation numerical interference even when the notation-shift was irrelevant.
Semantic neighborhood density had opposite effects on word processing: near neighbors slowed processing while distant neighbors sped it up, mediated by attractor dynamics in semantic space.
Phonological similarity between to-be-remembered items and irrelevant sounds disrupted free recall but not serial recall, when the similarity enabled category-cueing for retrieval.

Practicing only one operation (multiplication or factoring) produced interference with the other operation, while practicing both produced facilitative transfer in both directions—reflecting adaptive specialization versus flexibility.
